data.title: BDJ: Healthy-Looking Distribution With Value-Oriented Tilt
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund (BDJ) continues to deliver an attractive monthly distribution to its investors. This is a closed-end fund that focuses more on a value-oriented portfolio, which places the largest weightings into financials and healthcare, over technology. On top of this, the fund then employs a call writing strategy on the individual names within the underlying portfolio. That helps to generate option premiums to pay for that level of monthly payout. The current distribution rate is relatively moderate, but that bodes well for the sustainability at the present time.

BDJ Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.03
  • Discount/Premium: -5.99%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.15%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.88%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.722 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

BDJ's primary objective is to "provide current income and current gains." The fund intends to achieve this by "investing in common stocks that pay dividends and have the potential for capital appreciation." They concentrate on dividend-paying stocks, with "80% of its total assets in dividend-paying equities."

Performance - Discount Remains Appealing

Since our last update, the fund's total returns have been rather attractive on an absolute basis. Relative to the S&P 500 Index, it doesn't look as appealing.

However, the S&P 500 Index isn't its benchmark—instead, the fund focuses more on the Russell 1000 Value Index as its reference benchmark. Against the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), the fund has performed more similarly. The fund lagged this benchmark slightly, but that can often be seen when a covered call strategy is employed while the underlying portfolio is seen as rising at a fairly healthy clip.

When incorporating the long-term results of the fund, they also utilize the MSCI USA Value Call Overwrite Index as a benchmark as well. Against that benchmark, the fund has often exceeded the results seen there.

In general, technology and, primarily, the Magnificent 7 names have driven strong returns for the last several years now within the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Indexes; with that said, there is a case that can be made that they've become stretched in terms of valuation. It is true, they generate more meaningful earnings growth, but they are priced for perfection.

Once they start to stumble or the market sees something they don't like, they can quickly see a price correction. We've seen that with Meta Platforms (META) more recently. Even more broadly, we've been seeing companies like NVIDIA (NVDA) trade more sideways, causing the Nasdaq to pause while the Dow Jones Industrial Average taps a new all-time high.

BDJ can help to be a diversification away from a heavy tech-leaning portfolio that investors can often get when simply investing in the S&P 500 Index. Further, BDJ continues to trade at a bit of a discount to its net asset value—another key factor when considering a CEF in the first place. It has narrowed a touch since our prior update, but remains right around our 6% discount 'Buy' target.

Over the longer term, the fund is currently trading a bit above its average level. However, there have also been periods where the fund has flirted with a premium—even if only relatively briefly.

Distribution Looking Healthy

At the end of last year, they announced an increase in the monthly payout amount. That took it from $0.0562 per month to $0.0619. The NAV distribution rate comes in at 7.67%, which is fairly healthy, since the fund has to earn that plus the operating expenses in order to 'cover' the distribution being paid. That isn't an elevated rate, but thanks to the discount, the fund's share price distribution yield comes in a bit higher at 8.15%.

When considering a payout being covered, this is an equity-focused fund with a covered call writing strategy, so we aren't going to see income cover the payout alone. That is, the fund's net investment income that is generated from dividends paid in the underlying portfolio will not be able to support to 7.67% rate needed.

As of the last semi-annual report, NII per share came in at $0.09—working out to around 24% NII distribution coverage. The remainder was then made up from unrealized/realized capital gains. That figure will also include the options writing strategy of the fund, which can create losses or gains.

BDJ's Portfolio

The target overwrite portion for the covered calls is 30-40%, with the latest overwrite being listed at 47.88%, which indicates they are being more aggressive with trying to collect more option premiums. This was similar to when we last gave the fund a look when it was around 50% overwritten. So the target range seems to be fairly loose here.

With that said, while the fund may collect greater option premiums, the higher the overwrite, the higher the portion of the portfolio is subject to the upside cap. An upside cap refers to when a share price rises materially and breaches the strike price selected. So there is always a trade-off to consider.

That can be seen in the latest semi-annual report, where the fund actually realized around $10.227 million in losses due to the options written. This can occur when the covered call is closed or rolled at a loss to participate in further upside potential in terms of share price rising.

In terms of the underlying portfolio, as we noted, it leans toward financials and healthcare as the largest sector allocations. However, we have seen some slight shifts since our last update. Financials came down a touch from the 23.78% weighting, and healthcare also slipped a bit from the 15.91% level. Industrials were at 13.08%, and that also saw a small reduction. Information technology's weighting stayed nearly identical from the 12.33% allocation.

Still, the overall tilt toward value remains a rather consistent characteristic of the fund, which can make it a good diversifier for investors.

Drilling down to the top positions in the fund, we have seen some slight shifts here—most likely normal percentage shifts due to natural gyrations in share prices. For that, Wells Fargo (WFC) remains the largest holding, followed by Citigroup (C) and Cardinal Health (CAH), exactly as we previously saw but simply in different percentage weights.

One point of interest, though, is that Amazon (AMZN) has made its way to the top holding list, and previously, Microsoft (MSFT) was listed in the top ten. Going back to the semi-annual report, as of June 30, 2025, we can see AMZN and MSFT were both listed in the top ten at the time.

At that time, AMZN was 2.5% of the fund's breakdown and with 189,384 shares. Going back further to the N-PORT for quarterly holdings on March 30, 2025, AMZN was a position, but only with a 1% weight and 89,784 shares being held.

This seemed to be a good move to add AMZN shares, as even more recently, the earnings for AMZN being announced sent shares sharply higher—a stark contrast to META's shares. To be fair, META was held in BDJ, but at around $8.6 million and 11,670 shares at the time of its last semi-annual report, it wasn't a meaningful stake at only around 0.5% of the fund.

For MSFT, the shares have been moving pretty much flat for the last three months. They had previously reduced their position in MSFT earlier this year, and now not being shown in the top ten would further indicate they probably sold off some or all of their position there.

Conclusion

BDJ remains an attractive closed-end fund that continues to deliver a solid distribution to shareholders each month. Perhaps more importantly for some investors, this appears also to be a healthy distribution rate that seems sustainable. The fund also trades at around our 'Buy' level discount, even if it has narrowed a touch since our prior update.

data.title: Crescent Capital BDC: Attractive BDC, But More Downside Could Be Likely
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to the BDC (BIZD) sector, I've grown quite cautious due to macro uncertainty and falling interest rates. Although this has made BDCs more attractive from a valuation standpoint, I suggest for investors to proceed with caution.

Despite some displaying attractive discounts to their net asset values, I see no significant upside and a higher likelihood of seeing more downside in the near future. And this is due to expected lower base rates.

In this article, I discuss Crescent Capital BDC's (CCAP), latest earnings, fundamentals, and why investors should remain cautious despite the discount to NAV.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Crescent Capital BDC this past May in an article titled: Latest Quarter A Clear Sign Of A Weakening Economy.

As a result of macro uncertainty, I downgraded the BDC from a buy to a hold. Since, CCAP is down roughly 8.50% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up over 15% at the time of writing.

As a result of the deteriorating economic fundamentals, the BDC experienced sequential declines in both total and net investment income.

Non-accruals increased, while NAV declined, resulting in a 20% discount. Despite this, I wanted to see more fundamental stability and economic clarity before considering them a buy again.

Latest Quarter

CCAP recently reported their Q3 earnings for what I considered a disappointing quarter. While lower base rates played a role, I didn't like to see their total investment income decline from the prior quarter and year. Tll amounted to $41.3 million, down from $43 million in Q2 and $51.6 million the prior year.

On a positive note, their bottom line was flat from the prior quarter at $0.46, giving them solid dividend coverage of 110%. Moreover, the BDC has met or exceeded their base dividend for 39 consecutive quarters, something commendable considering the tougher economic backdrop.

Year-over-year, their bottom line declined a sizable 28% from $0.64. And while I think CCAP's dividend will remain safe for the near-term, 2026 will likely prove to be tougher for BDCs with rates expected to be lower.

And as a result, I suspect many BDCs could see further price declines. So, despite some trading at discounts currently, by the first half of 2026 is when I'm personally looking at adding BDCs to my portfolio again.

For comparison, other notable BDCs experienced steep declines in net investment income as well. Ares Capital (ARCC), the largest BDC, saw a $0.06 decline year-over-year from $0.54 to $0.48. As a result, dividend coverage fell to 100%.

Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) saw theirs decline $0.09 from $0.91 to $0.82. FSK KKR Capital (FSK) saw the largest drop of $0.20 from $0.77 to $0.57. And with lower rates, investors should expect further drops, resulting in potential dividend reductions.

Credit Quality

Regarding their overall credit quality, despite their NAV decline during the quarter, I think CCAP's credit quality is solid. NAV was impacted by the addition of two borrowers added to non-accrual status.

This brought these to 3.3% at cost and 1.6% at fair value, up from 1.7% and 0.9% respectively. While these aren't necessarily alarming, these were a little higher than I prefer to see.

For comparison, peer Hercules Capital's (HTGC) non-accruals were a conservative 1.2% at cost and 1.1% at fair value.

CCAP's management stated these were companies that sourced & shipped materials internationally. As a result, these had higher impacts from tariffs, resulting in realized losses of $0.15.

NAV stood at $19.28, down from $19.55 the previous quarter and down from $20.20 the prior year. In terms of portfolio performance, borrowers had a performance rating of 87%, a slight drop from 90% the previous year.

The BDC does have high first-lien exposure that does give them a more defensively-positioned portfolio. Their debt was also solidly staggered over the next couple of years. And liquidity was roughly $240 million in revolver capacity and $28 million in cash.

Leverage was above the peer average of 1.19x at 1.23x. But was still within management's targeted range of 1.1x - 1.3x. So, this does give them room for higher investment activity and provide temporary relief to the dividend should Nll continue to fall.

Additionally, they did have spillover of $1.10 that could also provide temporary relief and help them navigate the lower rate environment.

Attractive Discount

At the current price, this gives Crescent Capital BDC a 29.5% discount to NAV at the time of writing. Seeing this and their 12.4% yield using the annual base dividend run rate of $1.68, income-focused investors may consider CCAP a buy here.

But as previously mentioned, I don't see any significant upside and a higher likelihood for them to see downside due to lower base rates. One thing to note is CCAP's current discount is higher than their 5-year average, likely due to increased uncertainty. However, I suggest investors remain cautious going forward.

Risks

If the recent jobs report is any indication, then the economy could be headed for further weakening, or a potential recession. I've stated in prior articles that I think chances are increasing.

And unless we see stability in jobs in the next few months, I think we could see a potential downturn in the next 12 to 18 months. If so, this could lead to deteriorating fundamentals for BDCs as borrowers could face financial distress.

And this would result in increased leverage, tighter dividend coverage, higher non-accruals, and potential rises in payment-in-kind income. All of which would have a negative impact on BDC share prices.

Investor Takeaway

Despite declining financials, CCAP's solid dividend coverage and solid fundamentals amidst economic uncertainty are testaments to their resilience.

Moreover, their sizable discount and double-digit yield are attractive if you're an investor strictly looking for income.

However, further economic weakening and lower base rates are likely to result in additional downside. As a result of this, I, keeping Crescent Capital BDC at a hold.

data.title: NGL Energy Partners: Attractive Growth Proposal As Water Business Ramps Up
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Units of NGL Energy Partners (NGL) have risen sharply over the past few months as it booked a large amount more in the water solutions segment. This segment has been the pivot for the company over the past few years. It’s a high-margin business, and the scalability of it has been underappreciated by the market. NGL is allowed to double capacity, which will have a similar effect on the bottom line.

Previous critiques against the company have been the large debt position it has, now about 2.5x the market cap. But I’d argue that issue goes out the window as soon as this water solutions business begins to snowball further. The company is also selling off the natural gas segment's assets at a rapid pace, which has had a noticeable impact on the internal margins. I’m rating the stock a Strong Buy as I make the case that the disconnect between the stock price and the true value is unjustified, but offers a golden opportunity in the markets now.

Operational Overview

In NGL there are 3 distinct segments. The company has revamped their operations and is focusing now on being a predominantly water solutions business, rather than a midstream business buying and transporting crude oil and natural gas liquids. The shift and margin impact this has had is likely what has fueled the rally during 2025.

Water Solutions

This is now the bread and butter of the company, it will likely grow even further in terms of % of total revenue and EBITDA over the next few years.

Over the past 12 months, the revenues have climbed by 15.4% for the segment. Most of this is from the disposal service fees and sales of recovered crude oil, often also referred to as skim oil. What NGL basically does here is offer transportation, treatment, and recycling of water, which is used in crude oil and natural gas production. It's a considerable by-product that can’t just be dumped anywhere, and recovering it actually helps the oil producers as well, keeping costs down. In total, NGL has about 90 facilities across the US and about 194 disposal wells. Revenues are largely dependent on the overall production and market sentiment around oil and gas production. When times are good, volumes are up.

>90% of volume is committed via acreage dedications and MVCs with average remaining tenor of ~9 years

The revenues in this segment are also heavily contracted with an average span of 9 years. This creates long-term reliable revenue generation and visibility for the company. With a 90% committed level, NGL also ensures it always has revenues coming in, no matter what the customers deliver to them. Minimum volume commitments combined are 1030 mbbl/d.

Crude Oil Logistics

The second segment is crude oil, logistics. Here NGL focuses on buying oil directly from producers and then marketing and transporting this to refineries. Its most important pipeline is the Grand Mesa which runs about 550 miles from the DJ Basin to Oklahoma. Volumes during the last quarter were about 72,000 b/d compared to 63,000 b/d last year.

Natural Gas Liquids

The third segment is the natural gas liquids segment, which is what NGL is diverting away from the most right now. During the first quarter of the year, it held $32 million in assets here for sale. Most of this was in inventories, property, and goodwill. During the year the company has sold assets for about $270 million.

CEO Michael Krimbill commented on the sales:

These asset sales reduce the volatility and seasonality of our adjusted EBITDA and working capital requirements. The proceeds will be used to pay off the remaining ABL balance and the excess cash will be used for additional deleveraging and addressing other parts of our capital structure

The plan seems sound. NGL does hold a considerable amount of debt, $2.9 billion in total, which equates to about $263 million in interest expenses over the past 12 months. Pivoting into higher margin business will over time help deleverage as the working capital requirements go down with these asset sales. For reference, the sales covered 17 gas liquids terminals.

Not unsurprisingly, the revenues for the segment fell YoY to $244 million, down from $330 million. Most of the sales are from butane sales. I expect that the company will have to find a solution to reduce the inventory levels, as the total value is about $118 million, with $52 million being butane. Most inventory items are related to the liquid logistics segment. As part of the divestitures, I’d like to see the inventory levels also go down.

When comparing the EBITDA margins, it's easy to see why NGL is moving away from the segment. During the last report, it had a 4.3% adj. EBITDA margin, which might be an improvement from the 3.4% reported last year, is still far below what the water solutions segment is averaging.

Pivoting To Water Solutions

Over the past few years, the company has been pivoting into the water solutions business.

We are becoming more of a water solutions business with approximately 85% of our adjusted EBITDA to be generated by this segment.

The company CEO, Michael Krimbill highlighted this earlier this year in the Q4 2024 report. The 85% figure mentioned back then remained largely the same this past quarter as well, $151 million in adj. EBITDA from the segment, out of the total $178 million generated. The margin impact of this pivot has been nothing but impressive. In Q3 2022 the company had 5.73% in EBITDA margins. It’s now risen to 20% for the last quarter. During Q3 2022 the water solutions segment accounted for 73% of the adj. EBITDA. This clearly displays that pivoting into this segment is working, it’s raising the overall margins and creating a fundamentally much stronger business. I have to admit that seeing the falling revenues over the past few years without first researching and learning about the context had me worried. I now see a business transforming into a serious cash generating machine.

The company operates in a few different regions in the US. It has key exposure to the largest oil producing areas like the Permian, specifically the Delaware Basin here, and also Eagle Ford. When going over some of the most recent reports I could find for the production levels, it seems that the most growth is in the Delaware Basin. The rest of the regions all seem to be quite flat in terms of production. Granite Wash even declined somewhat from the year before, that being a few years ago, but it seems to be the most readily available report. Eaglebine, which is just northeast of the Eagle Ford region, is quite small, and there are no official figures as to the production levels.

US oil production rose by 2% in 2024, largely because of growing global demand for oil. Expectations are that the US will reach a new all-time-high in terms of oil production, which will negatively impact the prices, as oil prices are forecasted to decline to $50 per barrel by next year. Since NGL is pivoting away from more commodity price-driven revenues, it sort of gets to have the cake and eat it too here. Its main revenue source now being from water solutions, after a few years of piloting, means that it produces far more reliable revenues and carries less volatility.

Future Revenue Projection

I want to preface here that the growth proposal is quite considerable for NGL when comparing the current capacity in use and the total allowed capacity. It could more than double its production if it manages to secure more customers and increase asset size.

As we can see, there has also been a strong increase in the service fee per barrel, which hasn't come with higher operating expenses. This shows demand growth as oil producers look for large scale water solutions to help support production hikes on the back of domestic and global demand. YoY volumes grew 4.5%, which seems like a realistic trajectory for NGL as it scales up further. The permitted capacity is also far ahead of the current active capacity, suggesting a lot of upside still.

The growth capital is calculated by using the figures from the last report, which included an additional 500,000 in barrels per day, which caused the company to increase growth capital from $60 million to $160 million. This comes out to $320 per additional bpd, but a more conservative assumption, which I’m using, is $225 as the additional capacity includes some new wells as well. The D&A is based on 255 million barrels processed during the quarter and a reported $55.5 million in depreciations. This comes out to $0.21 per barrel. I’ve decided to increase this incrementally by $0.01 to reflect additional assets through the growth capital allocations. Maintenance CapEx is based on the reported $16.572 million last quarter, which is $0.065 per barrel.

What the forecast reveals is that NGL could drive serious growth in terms of both EBITDA and FCF without raising the cost of operations that much. Growth capital is relatively cheap and would create incredibly strong future revenue streams. Remember that the contract length is about 9 years on average and 90% of the contracted pipeline would be used.

So even at 90% usage, the EBITDA and the FCF generation is substantial. My assumptions for additional capacity are also below the 500,000 that the company added in terms of contracting last quarter. Spreading out the annual volume growth like in my model, I think, provides a clear growth path for the company, and substantiates why the stock still holds a lot of upside.

For the forecasted EV I’ve made a few base assumptions. I think that NGL will continue to pay down the total debt by 3.275% on average over the next few years, the same percentage it has averaged since 2020. I also think that NGL will continue redeeming its Class D units at an aggressive rate. Over the past 6 months, it has redeemed class D units at a rate of about $128 million per year. This seems sustainable since FCF is very high and the company can afford it. I did not make any assumptions for the cash or the market cap since those two are vastly more difficult and unreliable to predict. Using today's values is the best approach.

My models show that NGL still trades at a considerable discount even after rising so sharply over the past few months, partly because the previous bull case about high scalability in the water solutions business has come to fruition. I still think there is a lot of upside, and for a company set on generating over $1 billion in EBITDA in just a few years and nearly the same in free cash flows as well, and just valued at $1.19 billion in terms of market, makes it a screaming buy. The model is also made using the 90% usage case, which acts as a bare minimum in terms of EBITDA and FCF, at full capacity, the value here is even greater.

There is a disconnect between future value and the current unit price. I am planning on taking advantage of this as I rate the stock a strong buy.

Risks

The primary risk with NGL is the debts it has. As of the last report, it's $2.9 billion, and as was outlined in the model above here, the cash interest is substantial. It mutes a lot of the earnings it would otherwise generate. With a pivot towards a more profitable business segment like water solutions, a lot of the previous risks, I think, are mitigated. Higher margins provide more room to work in. The 4.3% EBITDA margins that Liquid Logistics has are not a sustainable level to operate on, especially when there is a lot of volatility involved too from commodity prices.

The Full Picture

NGL is rapidly increasing the capacity in the water solutions segment. This comes off the back of strong showings from the Delaware Basin within the Permian. Other regions like Eagle Ford remain stable, but the production hikes here are pushing more contracted water handling towards NGL. Last quarter's 500,000 contracted water handling was a show of strength and proof that the company can scale further. Don’t sleep on this one; it still has substantial upside potential.

data.title: Gladstone Investment: Q2 Earnings Confirms Positive Outlook (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

This challenging interest rate environment requires investors to be very meticulous about which business development company they maintain exposure to. Many BDCs are facing challenges when it comes to portfolio health and growing their underlying NAV. However, there are some BDCs that have demonstrated their strength through the chaos. I believe that Gladstone Investment (GAIN) has proven its ability to remain strong, despite some of its uncertainty. When I previously covered GAIN, I issued a hold rating due to the very high non-accruals and thin dividend coverage. I wanted to revisit GAIN now that its Q2 earnings have been reported.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that GAIN's share price has increased by about 5.1%. The fund's price has remained a lot more resilient than some peers in the sector. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return jumps up to 16.8% over the same time frame. GAIN now offers investors a starting dividend yield of around 6.8% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. This makes GAIN an enticing choice for investors seeking a high-income generation within their portfolio. Although GAIN has an excellent history of keeping payouts similar, I can't help but remain cautious about the distribution sustainability due to the tight earnings coverage.

Despite the thin dividend coverage, there are many aspects of GAIN that have improved over the last quarter. For instance, non-accruals have drastically declined, the NAV has increased, and capital has flowed into new investments. Therefore, a position in GAIN is becoming a lot more compelling at this time. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that GAIN implements to generate its earnings.

Q2 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio overview, GAIN has total investments at fair value of $1.13B that are spread across 28 different portfolio companies. GAIN generates its earnings by lending capital to middle market borrowers and collecting interest or equity in exchange. In order to generate its earnings, the portfolio takes on three primary debt structures, while leaning most heavily on secured first lien debt. This is ideal because first lien loans sit at the top of the corporate capital structure and have the highest priority for repayment. This structure helps protect GAIN from losing all invested capital in a bad transaction. The full breakdown by debt type is as follows:

  • Secured First Lien Debt: 53%
  • Preferred & Common Equity: 39%
  • Secured Second Lien Debt: 8%

As we can see, GAIN also has a sizeable exposure to equity investments. These may not produce the highest or most consistent level of cash flows, but they allow GAIN to participate in the growth of its underlying investments. However, equity investments are considered to be the riskiest since these sit at the bottom of the corporate capital structure and have the lowest priority for repayment.

Unlike other BDCs, GAIN takes a more focused approach to its investments. As we can see below, GAIN's portfolio only has exposure to three different industries: Manufacturing, Business/Consumer Services, and Consumer Products. This tighter focus can be both a strength or weakness for the portfolio, depending on market conditions.

GAIN reported its Q2 earnings at the beginning of November, and I believe the results were solid. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.24 per share, which was in-line with the prior quarter. Total investment income amounted to $25.2M, which was a slight 7.4% increase over the prior quarter's total investment income of $23.5M. Additionally, GAIN was able to see a $1M increase in total interest income collected from its positions, as well as a $0.7M increase in dividend and success fee income.

However, total expenses for the quarter saw a large increase of 45.3%, amounting to $21M for the quarter. The increased expenses can be attributed to capital gains-based incentive fees and an increase in interest expenses. As a result, net investment income was only $4.27M.

However, the most notable aspect of the last earnings report was the net increase in net assets of $28.7M, representing a 269.4% increase from the prior quarter. The increase can mostly be attributed to the large net unrealized appreciation of $54.3M during the quarter, indicating strength in its positions at this time. Overall, I think this was a solid quarter that helped establish some positive momentum for GAIN's earnings potential.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since GAIN is structured as a BDC, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, GAIN now trades at a premium to NAV of 3.40%. For reference, GAIN has traded at an average premium to NAV of 6.69% over the last five years. To better visualize the current premium in relation to its historical range, we can refer to the red line on the graph below. As we can see, GAIN now trades at a more reasonable valuation that may justify the accumulation of shares.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $14.13 per share, which indicates there's a small ~1% upside from the current level. I believe that this sort of growth is certainly possible when considering the growth that has occurred over the quarter. For instance, management was able to invest $71M into new opportunities during the quarter, which can drive earnings growth once these investments materialize. Additionally, total dollars repaid and collected from sales amounted to $1.4M, ultimately leading to healthy NAV growth over the quarter. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q3 2024: $13.30 per share
  • Q4 2024: $13.55 per share
  • Q1 2025: $12.99 per share
  • Q1 2025: $13.53 per share

From a risk perspective, GAIN's overall portfolio health has slightly improved. For instance, management has confirmed on the last earnings call, that the amount of portfolio companies on non-accrual status have been reduced from four down to three for the quarter. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. As a result, these portfolio companies aren't actively contributing towards GAIN's earnings and reduce earnings potential. Non-accruals now sit at 3.9% at cost and 1.7% at fair portfolio value.

Following these improvements, GAIN's non-accruals look better and are now closely aligned with some more notable peers in the BDC sector. During a time when many BDCs are experiencing an uptick in non-accruals, it's great to see that GAIN has been able to improve its health. For reference, here are some of the non-accrual rates for peer BDCs:

  • PennantPark Investment (PNNT): 0.7% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Barings BDC Inc. (BBDC): 0.4% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Since the Fed initiated its second 25 basis point cut to interest rates this year, I believe that GAIN will be able to continue capitalizing on lower rates to drive growth. A lower cost of debt has the possibility of stimulating an influx of new companies seeking debt financing, which provides GAIN with plenty of opportunities to invest in new portfolio companies. Additionally, a lower cost of debt may improve the operating spread for GAIN's portfolio companies, which can improve non-accruals.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.8%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by three months, we get a quarterly payout of $0.24 per share. As previously mentioned, adjusted net investment income amounted to $0.24 per share for the quarter. So while earnings does support the current payout, there is very little margin for error. If non-accruals were to increase or management fails to grow its portfolio during a quarter, the net investment income can easily drop below the payout amount.

After observing GAIN for several years now, the dividend coverage has always remained tight. However, management always reassures shareholders that they are confident in their ability to continue supporting the current payouts. I suspect this is due to the remaining spillover income that management can tap into if needed. Management only tracks their spillover income on an annual basis, so they started the year with $1.50 per share.

We started the year with $1.50, which covers the supplemental of $0.54 in June and each month of $0.08. So we really have only been eating into the current spillover that we started the year with. So we still feel comfortable and are confident in where we're going to end the year. - Taylor Ritchie, CFO

Additionally, GAIN has paid out large supplementals over the last few years. Management has confirmed that they seek to continue paying out large supplementals when possible. Since there is still spillover income available it is certainly possible that another supplemental is declared before the end of the year.

However, it's important for investors to know that the distributions received from GAIN offer no tax efficiency. Since distributions are fueled by net investment income, the income received would typically be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in GAIN would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on GAIN to a buy at this time. GAIN's portfolio has remained strong as non-accruals have improved, earnings have remained consistent, and management continues to allocate capital towards new investments. Additionally, GAIN's NAV increased over the quarter, is a clear sign of things starting to improve. Although the dividend coverage remains thin, management has confirmed their confidence to continue supporting payouts due to the remaining spillover income and ability to grow the baseline earnings within the portfolio through new investments.

data.title: MongoDB Q3 Earnings Preview: It's Time For A Change In Strategy
data.description:
After a spectacular Q2 earnings report a couple of months ago, the date for MongoDB's (NASDAQ:MDB) Q3 2026 report is fast approaching. The company is scheduled to report it on 1st of December, which is right after the Thanksgiving holiday weekend.

Investors' reaction to the Q2 report quickly brought MDB to new highs for the year and since then the stock has retained its gains and continued to perform roughly in-line with the market. This was in stark contrast to the reaction to the Q4 2025 report in March when the stock fell sharply.

Although the sharp jump in August was a breath of fresh air for investors, MDB is still just barely above the Nasdaq Composite index for the past 3-year period. On top of that, as we could see on the graph below, MDB share price exhibits extremely high volatility which makes quarterly earnings reactions far less impressive for long-term investors.

MongoDB's stock is also subject to very high market risk with a beta coefficient that is now above 4. Meaning that investors should expect returns of more than four times those of the equity market, in order to be compensated for market risk.

All that highlights the importance of evaluating share price performance through the lens of risks and avoid falling victim to emotional biases stemming from positive market reactions following one earnings release. With that out of the way, we could now take a closer look at what happened during the last quarter and pinpoint some key areas that investors should focus on during the upcoming release.

Profitability In The Spotlight

Since I first covered MDB in 2024 I have been highlighting the issues related to the company's GAAP profitability. Even though the last quarterly report was well-perceived by investors it did little to dispel some of these fears that MDB might have serious difficulties becoming a highly profitable entity on a GAAP basis.

The headline of the Q2 report was quite impressive with a massive beat both on the top and bottom line. This was enough to get some investors excited about the future and to ignore the significant Non-GAAP adjustments.

Management guidance for the full fiscal year was also increased from an initially expected Non-GAAP Earnings Per Share to be within the range of $2.94 to $3.12, to the newly provided range of $3.64 to $3.73 or an increase of almost 22%.

Nonetheless, the gross margin continued falling and is now at its lowest levels since 2023. The recent drops have completely offset improvements registered over the past two years. This is a concerning trend for a company that is undergoing a period of unprecedented demand for its services which usually results in higher pricing power.

Another ongoing issue for MDB is the level of Non-GAAP adjustments and their dynamic. Although Non-GAAP EPS skyrocketed and the guidance for the full fiscal year was increased, the GAAP results went in the other direction. The net loss on a GAAP basis widened during the last quarter and with that the level of Non-GAAP adjustments is now near record-high levels.

These adjustments are primarily related to stock-based compensation packages, but in Q2 2025 period we also had restructuring expenses, amortization of intangibles related to the recent acquisition of Voyage AI and income tax adjustments. That's not a positive signal that MDB is headed to achieving GAAP profitability, but quite the opposite.

The good news is that cash flow from operations improved significantly on a year-on-year basis, but due to the significant stock-based compensation expense, the gap with operating income remains high.

Even though cash flow is arguably a more useful metric for shareholders, MDB still trades at a ridiculously high cash flow multiple.

The same is true for MDB's earnings multiples on historical and forward Non-GAAP basis.

As a result, although MDB management just recently announced that they expect the company to deliver results at the high end of the previously provided guidance, the share price is down since the announcement was made.

Based on preliminary, unaudited results, the Company expects to exceed the high end of the provided third quarter fiscal year 2026 guidance for revenue, non-GAAP income from operations, and non-GAAP earnings per share, driven by continued strength in Atlas.

Source: MongoDB Website

The Management Change

During the same announcement from about a month ago, it also became clear that the CEO who took MongoDB public all the way back in 2017 will be stepping down and a replacement has been found. It is still too early to speculate on the exact reasons and the expected change under the new management, but it is highly likely for MDB to change course over the coming years.

To me personally, this sends a strong signal that the board of directors has finally recognized that a major change in strategy is needed and I would not rule out a potential M&A deal down the line.

All that once again confirms that the current strategy based on organic growth is not working and MDB's management needs to either expand more aggressively or look for a larger company to acquire it.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Another confirmation that MDB management might be working towards such a scenario came earlier this year after one of its major peers - Couchbase was acquired by a larger company. With the other major peer - ArangoDB, operating as a privately held company, this leaves MDB as the only pure-play publicly traded NoSQL database provider.

The probability that MDB would not be able to realize the necessary economies of scale in order to be highly profitable on a GAAP basis has now increased significantly and in my view this is what's going to drive the major shift in strategy.

The company has been growing very fast in recent years and expected revenue growth rate remains at around 20%, but even after all that the level of fixed cost to sales remains prohibitively high at above 80%. What's even worse is that this ratio has increased even further during the last two reports.

That is why, investors should keep a close eye on the level of fixed costs during the upcoming release. As results are anticipated to come at the high-end of the previously provided guidance, one would assume that this ratio should come down significantly in Q3.

Investor Takeaway

After reporting much better Non-GAAP results in Q2, MDB is on track of yet another strong quarter next week. While results are expected to come at the high-end of the previous guidance (or maybe even above it), a repeat of investors' reaction to the Q2 release appears less likely. Investors should also look for any clues of a potential strategy shift under the new CEO, alongside signs that Non-GAAP adjustments and the share of fixed costs to sales could come down drastically.

data.title: Kratos: Strong Quarter, But Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Thesis

Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS), Inc. is an American company specializing in the production of low-cost aircraft, mostly drones. The quarterly results were very good. A few months ago I wrote an analysis on the stock and gave it my "hold" rating.

The defense sector is a cyclical industry. Perhaps once upon a time. Today, in 2025, the narrative has changed. I'm European, and I learned this from the Americans. Investing in military defense is no longer optional. A country's sovereignty is only guaranteed if it possesses more precise and powerful weapons than its adversary. They don't necessarily have to be used, but they do act as a deterrent to enemies.

Earnings Season (Q3 2025)

I've already discussed the company's business in a previous analysis, so I won't dwell on this aspect in detail. I'm more interested in analyzing the company's recent quarterly results, which beat estimates, but something didn't quite work.

We all know it: when earnings are released, investors are ruthless. If all the numbers are positive—revenue and EBITDA are rising, guidance is rising—but even just one figure doesn't please the market, then the stock gets sold. And that's what happened to Kratos Defense.

Yet the quarterly results showed truly strong numbers. Earnings and revenues exceeded expectations, and by no small amount. Yet the next day, the stock was down about 15% from its highs.

What worked

Both revenues and EBITDA were beaten; we're talking about $347.6 million in revenues, 7.72% above estimates, and $30.8 million in EBITDA versus the estimated $25-$30 million.

Guidance also surprised on the upside, both for 2025 and the following two years; We're talking about growth of 14-15% (estimated 11-13%) for 2025, 15-20% for 2026, and even 18-23% for 2027. This trend is also confirmed by the increase in margins; in fact, for 2026 and 2027, management expects an expansion of 100 basis points in the EBITDA margin.

Growth is also being driven by Poseidon, a military hardware and systems program. It's not yet clear which components are being discussed, whether ground-based or air-based. This information is still classified, but it's estimated that the project could be worth up to $750 million.

Along with this contract, other projects are mentioned: Demons (SLCM_N), Anaconda, and Helios.

There are projects that are still premature, but that, according to Eric DeMarco, CEO of Kratos, could be worth billions by 2028. The business is driven by autonomous drones, but the company has its hands full with other projects.

What Didn't Work

From this perspective, the quarterly results are a strong buy for Kratos Defense, but there are negative aspects that are having a significant impact (and in fact, the market has penalized the stock).

The company, in fact, is burning cash and has FCF that remains weak and unreassuring. During the call with analysts, management said that cash flow will remain negative for a few more years (not before the end of 2027). Finally, margins are still very complex.

The company is suffering from sales of fixed-cost products, at least for another two years. This means that an increase in the cost of a material or the price of a subcontractor directly impacts the company's margins. This implies that at least until 2027, the company is highly sensitive to these variations.

In my previous analysis, I praised the fact that a factory had been opened in Oklahoma, but currently volumes are still low because the capacity is not yet fully utilized. Therefore, fixed costs impact the low volumes produced, once again reducing the company's margins.

And again, the market hasn't looked favorably on the fact that Kratos is bidding on billion-dollar contracts, but preparing for a tender of this caliber is enormously expensive. These non-capitalizable costs end up in the income statement, reducing the company's margins.

Valuation

From a fundamental point of view, the situation is less good than the quarterly results. Although Q3 2025 raised guidance, the stock's multiples already reflect what was promised in the investor call, and perhaps even more: the stock is already priced in for 2028.

Kratos's P/E is 142x for 2025, dropping to 68x in 2027 and 54x in 2028, despite earnings growth of 40% in 2026 and 26% in 2028. The numbers speak for themselves: Kratos has strong earnings growth, but its P/E isn't decreasing at the same rate. Therefore, the market in 2025 is paying hyper-growth multiples for 2028.

I'm talking numbers and prices now to understand whether the price at least reflects the 2028 forecast and has room for growth. The 2028 adjusted EPS is $1.39 according to the consensus. Studying competing defense companies that also deal in autonomous aircraft, the average P/E is 49x 2028 earnings. So, if I were to apply this multiple to Kratos' EOS, I would obtain a company value of $68 per share, slightly lower than the current price. From my perspective, the stock doesn't seem undervalued, but I also don't think it makes sense to increase exposure. The valuation doesn't suggest an imminent collapse, so if you already own the stock in your portfolio with a CPA below the current price, a hold seems reasonable. The stock's price already incorporates much of the defense boom and future contracts, effectively leaving little room for error. That's part of the game, and for this reason, while continuing to hold my shares, I'm maintaining a Hold rating.

Risks

First point: low margins

The stock is pricing in very high future growth. The P/E ratios tell us this, the capex tells us this, and the sales of fixed-price products tell us this. The market rewards Kratos for what it can do in the future rather than what it is already doing. This is normal; it happens with all companies, but if expectations are extremely high, it doesn't take much for it to fall.

Second point: defense trend

Kratos Defense has been driven by massive defense investments. We must be aware that if this trend were to slow, or a single project were to be delayed, the stock would move very high. And not positively.

Opportunities

It's not all negative news; the company is well-positioned, it's closing government contracts, it has a functioning division that drives the company forward, and others that are expanding in various areas.

It has a market capitalization of $12.512 million, which, for me as a company analyst, means it still has ample room for growth.

Conclusion

As I said before, I maintain my hold rating on the company. Kratos has ridden the right wave; it's a defense company at a time when there are too many wars. Military spending is very high and will continue to be significant, but there are weaknesses that cannot be ignored. The AI-powered autonomous drone sector is driving the company, and future projects certainly offer enormous potential.

The market has recently become more selective, separating companies that are cash-crunching and generating margins (currently) from those that thrive on contracts and short-term narratives (be they technology companies or, as in this case, defense companies) but are not yet solid (or are even burning cash). These types of companies have risen sharply in recent years (KTOS is +190% YTD), but I believe the market is dampening this euphoria, slowing this disproportionate growth.

I have the stock in my portfolio, and I think it makes sense to hold it, but if I were in the shoes of someone who decides to buy the stock today, perhaps I would avoid it. The stock is trading at excessively high price-earnings. Even when I bought it, the price-earnings were high, but the market context was different, the market was less demanding, and I prefer not to fall in love with companies that could be the first to be sold, precisely because they have grown too much.

Kratos Defense is a company that is capitalizing on market momentum with contracts already signed, but its margins, cash flow, and high P/E suggest to me that the market has already priced in the company's future growth.

The company is benefiting from the defense boom, but it still has some cost issues to resolve. Furthermore, it is entirely dependent on government contracts.

data.title: Wasatch Global Value Fund Q3 2025 Performance Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Wasatch Global Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

The Wasatch Global Value Fund—Investor Class gained 6.36% in the third quarter of 2025, outperforming the MSCI All Country (AC) World Value Index, which added 6.13%. Contributions to the Fund’s performance relative to the benchmark were led by positioning within the information technology (IT) and consumer-discretionary sectors, while positioning within the health care sector weighed most heavily on relative performance.

Leading contributors to the Fund’s performance included Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA). While the company posted better-than-expected earnings supported by growth in its cloud computing services segment after a mild disappointment in the prior quarter, this was a relatively small part of the quarter’s narrative. Alibaba has invested heavily in integrating AI across its e-commerce, logistics and cloud-computing ecosystems while developing its own large language model AI tool. The stock was initially boosted alongside the broader Chinese IT sector by news in July that NVIDIA (NVDA) would resume sales of its H20 AI chips to China. Subsequent reports that Alibaba had developed an AI processor to compete with NVIDIA’s product for the Chinese market, propelled the stock higher against the backdrop of investors’ enthusiasm for all things AI.

Pharmaceutical and medical-device company Johnson & Johnson (J&J) (JNJ) posted second quarter revenues and earnings that were better than expected. Management also increased guidance, signaling to investors conviction with respect to the long-term growth trajectory of J&J’s “innovative medicine” pharma segment. This growth is being fueled by the company’s pipeline of treatments in development as well as by acquisitions such as the $14.6 billion purchase of Intra-Cellular Therapies in January. As a result, we believe J&J is well-positioned relative to peers with respect to mitigating the impact on earnings from lost exclusivity as drugs run off patent.

Samsung Electronics Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) is a leading provider of dynamic random access memory (DRAM) chips widely used in PCs and mobile phones. The stock rose on optimism that Samsung is poised to benefit from demand for the ultrafast high bandwidth memory (HBM) chip architecture used in more advanced AI applications. Specifically, in the quarter Samsung’s most advanced DRAM chip, the HBM3E 12H, qualified for NVIDIA’s graphics processing unit (GPU) platform for AI inferencing. In addition, production volumes for Samsung’s foundry business are expected to receive a boost from demand for HBM chips.

Leading detractors from Fund performance for the quarter included Centene Corp. (CNC), an insurance provider with a large Medicaid business. Under the Trump Administration, all Medicaid plans offered through exchanges set up under the Affordable Care Act have been subject to changed determination guidelines aimed at improving “program integrity,” with the effect of reducing enrollment of healthier individuals. Centene pulled its full-year guidance entering the quarter due to lack of clarity around rising utilization rates and costs associated with its Ambetter Medicaid plan, leading to a sharp decline in its stock price. While subsequently announcing disappointing earnings in late July, Centene’s management stated that the company’s Medicaid offering was underpriced for the rising morbidity of enrollees. We believe the uncertainty around pricing will subside and Centene will be able to successfully address these challenges.

Amdocs Ltd. (DOX) is an IT consulting and services company that helps clients manage complex processes and enhance the customer experience. Amdocs posted solid results in the quarter while reporting a lower volume of unfilled orders. Investors appeared to be concerned that the reduced backlog was an indication that the company’s business model was coming under threat from AI. However, we found no evidence that the reduced backlog was the result of anything other than normal variability in the timing of new business wins. Amdocs is actively engaged in helping clients utilize AI to modernize their operations. We view the company as more likely to emerge as an “AI winner” than to have its services simply displaced by AI.

Sentiment softened with respect to German insurer Munich Re as the company posted earnings that were in line with expectations but were viewed as low quality, (i.e., the result of higher-than-expected investment income and having dodged catastrophic losses rather than being driven by strong underwriting). In this vein, the company missed revenue targets and modestly lowered guidance for its core reinsurance segment. More broadly, investors have been concerned that pricing power for property and casualty insurers is undergoing a cyclical waning after a period of strength. We view the weakness as part of the normal cyclicality of the insurance business and we’re comfortable maintaining our position. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

data.title: Wasatch Global Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

Global equity market returns were broadly positive in the third quarter of 2025, boosted by the outlook for declining U.S. interest rates and investors’ enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI).

U.S. equities added to gains seen over the first half of the year, supported by continued positive economic growth, an easing of fears over the impact of tariffs, and strong corporate earnings reports. In addition, the U.S. Federal Reserve lowered short-term rates by a quarter-point at its September meeting, a move widely interpreted as signaling the resumption of policy easing. This backdrop led the market to assume a “risk-on” tone led by the stocks of companies viewed as likely beneficiaries of spending related to AI.

Chinese stocks posted outsized returns on policy support for domestic chipmakers and accelerating spending on AI, along with easing trade tensions with the U.S. and steps by the government to address excess capacity in key industries. Japanese equities also rose notably on a trade deal with the U.S. and the outlook for central bank easing. Returns in the U.K. and Europe were also positive but more modest. German equities finished with a small gain for the quarter after hitting all-time highs earlier in the year. French stocks rose slightly despite a period of political instability.

The Wasatch Global Value Fund—Investor Class gained 6.36% in the third quarter of 2025, outperforming the MSCI All Country (AC) World Value Index, which added 6.13%. Contributions to the Fund’s performance relative to the benchmark were led by positioning within the information technology (IT) and consumer-discretionary sectors, while positioning within the health care sector weighed most heavily on relative performance.

While subsequently announcing disappointing earnings in late July, Centene’s management stated that the company’s Medicaid offering was underpriced for the rising morbidity of enrollees. We believe the uncertainty around pricing will subside and Centene will be able to successfully address these challenges.

Amdocs Ltd. (DOX) is an IT consulting and services company that helps clients manage complex processes and enhance the customer experience. Amdocs posted solid results in the quarter while reporting a lower volume of unfilled orders. Investors appeared to be concerned that the reduced backlog was an indication that the company’s business model was coming under threat from AI. However, we found no evidence that the reduced backlog was the result of anything other than normal variability in the timing of new business wins. Amdocs is actively engaged in helping clients utilize AI to modernize their operations. We view the company as more likely to emerge as an “AI winner” than to have its services simply displaced by AI.

Sentiment softened with respect to German insurer Munich Re as the company posted earnings that were in line with expectations but were viewed as low quality, (i.e., the result of higher-than-expected investment income and having dodged catastrophic losses rather than being driven by strong underwriting). In this vein, the company missed revenue targets and modestly lowered guidance for its core reinsurance segment. More broadly, investors have been concerned that pricing power for property and casualty insurers is undergoing a cyclical waning after a period of strength. We view the weakness as part of the normal cyclicality of the insurance business and we’re comfortable maintaining our position. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook

Equity valuations remain elevated, suggesting a degree of caution is warranted. That said, global liquidity is at record levels and that money continues to seek investment avenues, advising against taking an overly defensive posture in the Fund.

Throughout 2025, we’ve been evaluating opportunities in more economically sensitive stocks with the potential to benefit from central bank rate cuts while also weighing the risks to these stocks from any tariff-driven softening in economic conditions. Over the third quarter, we added exposure on the margins to what we see as attractively valued cyclical companies with high degrees of operating leverage (i.e., mostly fixed costs, meaning that increases in revenues largely flow to bottom-line earnings).

The Fund’s overall positioning remains somewhat defensive against an evolving global trade backdrop. As such, the Fund remains tilted toward lower volatility, higher dividend areas of the market such as financials, utilities, real estate investment trusts (REITs), telecommunications and large-cap pharmaceuticals. Looking ahead, we favor international markets relative to the U.S. and Europe relative to Asia, based largely on valuations and where monetary policy is most supportive.

As always, we will continue to adhere to our stock selection discipline regardless of the short-term direction of the global markets in response to a shifting macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop.

“Risk-on” is when investors are seeking the potentially higher returns of riskier assets and put money back in the market willing to risk the money, thus risk on.
